data.title: John Hancock Multimanager 2015 Lifetime Portfolio Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Highlights

  • The first quarter marked a reversal of the trends in place during 2024, with a poor showing for U.S. growth stocks but stronger returns for most other asset categories.
  • The John Hancock Multimanager 2015 Lifetime Portfolio (MUTF:JLBAX) produced a positive total return and outpaced the benchmark.
  • Asset allocation contributed to results, but the relative performance of the underlying managers detracted.

Market review and outlook

Uncertainty regarding U.S. tariffs led to a wide range of returns across the world financial markets in the first quarter. U.S. equities were one of the worst performing asset categories on concerns that Washington's trade policies could fuel resurgent inflation and depress economic growth. Additionally, worries about reduced investment in AI-related infrastructure caused a sell-off in U.S. technology stocks. Although the U.S. growth style was hit hard by these developments, the value category posted a gain as investors sought opportunities in areas that had underperformed in 2024. Developed- and emerging-market stocks also recorded positive returns at a time of U.S. dollar weakness and positive sentiment on the outlook for European fiscal spending.

In the fixed-income market, higher-quality and more interest rate-sensitive assets delivered solid gains at a time of falling inflation and renewed hopes that the U.S. Federal Reserve would ease policy later in the year. The credit sectors (including investment-grade corporates, high-yield bonds, bank loans, and the emerging markets) produced positive returns but didn't keep pace with U.S. Treasuries. The underperformance for these areas, which marked another reversal from 2024, reflected an uptick in investor risk aversion.

Contributors and detractors

During the past two years, our diversified approach didn't have the anticipated benefits due to the high concentration of returns in a very narrow group of U.S. technology stocks. This trend changed in the first quarter as shifting headlines prompted a rotation toward other areas of the financial markets. We believe the fund's outperformance in this shifting environment helps illustrate the benefits of our emphasis on using a broadly diversified and long-term approach, rather than one that tries to chase short-term performance.

Asset allocation contributed to performance in the first quarter. The fund benefited from its underweight in U.S. large-cap stocks and a corresponding overweight in defensive equities. The real assets portfolio also helped results thanks to gains for natural resource and infrastructure stocks. This segment of the fund is designed to act as an offset to concerns against rising inflation, and it proved effective in that regard over the first three months of 2025. On the other hand, an underweight in the international markets detracted given the category's meaningful outperformance. The fund also lost some relative performance from an underweight in bonds versus stocks. Underlying manager results detracted, with the weakest relative performance occurring on the fixed-income side.

Portfolio changes

At the close of the quarter, U.S. trade policy remained a significant wild card for the economy and financial markets. Our response to the uncertainty was to maintain a steady, balanced approach with small changes on the margin but no significant shifts. Instead of trying to be reactive to headlines, we strive to maintain a balanced approach. Balanced, in this case, isn't a traditional 60/40 stock/bond allocation. We instead look to keep the portfolios positioned for a broad range of outcomes for market drivers, such as inflation, growth, currency movements, and political developments. As always, we maintain a steady focus on the pillars of our investment process: valuations, fundamentals, and diversification. We believe the fund is well positioned to navigate the current environment through our flexibility to adjust the portfolio based on evolving conditions and capitalize on opportunities created by market volatility.

data.title: TCW Global Bond Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

A rise in volatility characterized the first quarter of 2024 as the new U.S. administration made clear that there would be a sequencing to campaign promises. Short term pain – a “detox period,” as described by the Treasury Secretary – would be a necessary step towards the achievement of longer-term aspirations, and the first order of business would be tariffs. Optimism for pro-growth policies gave way to concerns about the inflationary impact of tariffs on markets where inflation was still running higher than targets, while governmental efficiency and fiscal austerity programs weighed heavily on confidence. Assessments of both present and future conditions reached the lowest levels in years, including the potential for economic slowing as estimates of first quarter GDP indicated a substantially lower run rate than what market participants had become accustomed to. With souring sentiment and a broad risk-off tone to end the quarter, U.S. Treasuries rallied, led by the 43 basis point (bps) decline in the 5-Year, and the Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) opted to pause interest rate changes, advocating for a patient approach as it assesses the impact of tariffs. Amid policy uncertainty and recession concerns, the world’s largest reserve currency sank, with the DXY Index falling nearly 4.0%.

In Europe, policy divergence with the U.S. grew, with the European Central Bank (ECB) cutting rates by 25 bps in January and March, taking the policy rate to 2.5%. The quarter also witnessed a significant shift in U.S. – European Union (EU) relations, with the likelihood of economic and trade decoupling between the two regions increasing. In a historic departure from fiscal conservatism, Germany approved a €500 billion infrastructure package and eased its debt brake to facilitate increased defense expenditures. As a result of these developments, the Euro Stoxx 600 (+5.9%) ended the quarter with the largest outperformance gap versus the S&P 500 (-4.3%) in a decade, European yield curves steepened (led by a sell-off in the long end), and the Euro rallied 4.5% vs the U.S. dollar. Meanwhile, the United Kingdom (UK) faced ongoing challenges as the Bank of England (BoE) halved its annual growth forecasts, citing expectations for prolonged higher interest rates, a slowing labor market, and adverse tariff impacts. The BoE cut rates by 25 bps in February, but held rates steady in March, taking a wait-and-see approach given still high inflation and global trade uncertainties. Conversely, the Bank of Japan (BoJ) implemented its third rate-hike in January but paused in March to assess the effects of higher tariffs and international challenges on its recovery.

Against this backdrop, the Bloomberg Global Aggregate Index gained 2.6% during the quarter, largely driven by the U.S. component as the repricing of growth expectations and attendant decline in Treasury yields saw the reemergence of fixed income as a haven asset class. However, risk premiums increased across credit sectors, resulting in relative underperformance on a duration-adjusted basis. More specifically, global corporates gained 2.8%, but widened by 9 bps, while CMBS and ABS, up 2.6% and 1.8%, widened by 7 and 14 bps, respectively. Meanwhile, agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS) and covered bonds returned 3.1% and 4.7%, respectively, and were the only non-government fixed income spread sectors to see yield premiums decline.

Performance

The TCW Global Bond Fund I-Class (“Fund”) gained 3.62% in the first quarter of 2025 to finish ahead of the Bloomberg Global Aggregate Bond Index by 98 bps. Relative performance was driven by the continued duration overweight in the U.S. and emphasis on front-end maturities as rates reset lower and the curve steepened out alongside the repricing of Federal Reserve (Fed) policy. In addition, the short duration position in Japan and China further rewarded relative returns as yields rose in both countries over the quarter, and the underweight Japanese duration was reduced at more attractive valuations.

Meanwhile, long duration positions in the UK and Europe were a small drag on relative performance as intermediate and longer yields moved higher, however, the overweight to front-end UK yields contributed as these yields fell. Currency positioning was also additive to returns, with the Japanese yen (JPY) and Norwegian krone (NOK) overweights contributing as both currencies posted strong performance during the quarter. Additionally, the long Brazilian real (BRL) position contributed as the currency appreciated, and the position was closed out at attractive levels. From an allocation perspective, overweight positions across securitized products modestly weighed on relative returns as these sectors were not immune from the spread widening. However, disciplined security selection produced a sizable contribution that more than outweighed the sector drag, with the biggest contributions coming from CMBS backed by SASB deals and the allocation to non-traditional ABS like CLOs given a stream of steady income and attractive yield premiums. Meanwhile, the Fund had been positioned for a credit-led risk-off environment given a sizeable underweight to corporates, informed by the view that spreads remained too tight and provided little downside protection. This underweight insulated relative returns from the widening that occurred over the period, particularly among cyclicals and industries closely tied to global trade patterns, though issue selection among emerging market credits was a slight drag.

Outlook and Positioning

While there has been a brief reprieve (tariffs have been paused for 90 days on non-retaliating countries at the time of writing this), the erratic tariff development will likely keep volatility elevated in our view. Uncertainty remains high in terms of the global economic outlook, global capex, central bank reaction functions, potential domestic fiscal responses outside the U.S., and unintended consequences, including the potential for higher inflation in the U.S. and weaker inflation in the rest of the world (e.g., as China potentially releases their excess supply on Europe and the rest of the world). With tariff wars between the U.S. and the rest of the world a significant risk to the global economy, our positioning remains selective, and we continue to look for idiosyncratic opportunities and attractive relative value. The Fund maintains exposure across higher quality sovereign issuers with strong or improving macroeconomic outlooks that can weather volatility. Duration overweights are focused in the U.S. and the UK, as the Fed and Bank of England are both likely to have to cut rates more aggressively than what is currently priced in. China remains the largest duration underweight as the PBoC maintains easy monetary policy and better valuations can be found in other markets. Currency positioning remains underweight the U.S. dollar, with longs against the NOK, Euro, and Australian dollar. The NOK remains below fair value in our view despite a hawkish central bank, with further support from an already strong and still-improving current account surplus, while the Euro should also benefit from a growing current account surplus and Germany’s fiscal plans. Australia is expected to fare better than other countries with respect to U.S. tariffs, while an improving balance of payments, widening interest rate differentials between the U.S. and Australia, and expectations for USD weakness further support the currency.

Underscoring the discipline with which the TCW team manages portfolios and adheres to its value-oriented discipline, corporate credit remains an underweight position given spread levels that continue to trade rich relative to history. The widening that occurred among corporates late in the quarter was the first step in what is likely to be a remediation towards long-run average levels, though those moves appeared to be more of a capping of upside potential rather than a full reflection of downside risks. Until valuations more accurately reflect these risks and sector- and ratings-wide dispersion occurs, exposure remains focused on positions in high-conviction issuers and companies that will likely benefit from Germany’s fiscal expansion. We continue to actively evaluate opportunities to add risk that is more appropriately priced. Additionally, opportunities exist in below investment grade developed and emerging market credit, with allocations focused on issuers with strong or improving growth fundamentals. Securitized products present a more compelling relative value proposition given better spreads, in our view, informing a widespread overweight across sectors. Agency MBS constitutes the largest securitized position given its resilience during risk-off periods, favorable liquidity, and over 40 bps of spread premium versus investment grade credit. Though non-agency MBS doesn’t carry the government guarantee, the sector is supported by robust collateral profiles that help maximize cashflow back to bondholders, with the ongoing evolution of structures and deal types across the sector fertile ground to capture alpha potential at the issue level. Similarly, CMBS exposure is focused on SASB deals that allow for a detailed analysis of the underlying property and sponsor, facilitating bottom-up inclusion criteria that includes higher yielding positions down the capital structure. ABS positions similarly reflect targeted allocations, with European CLOs comprising the bulk of the exposure and smaller positions in ABS backed by shipping containers, data centers, and select FFELP student loans.

The performance data presented represents past performance and is no guarantee of future results. Returns assume all income items are reinvested. Glossary of Terms

ABS (Asset-Backed Securities)– A financial security backed by a loan, lease or receivables against assets other than real estate and mortgage-backed securities. Accommodative Monetary Policy– When a central bank (such as the Federal Reserve) attempts to expand the overall money supply to boost the economy when growth is slowing (as measured by GDP). Agency MBS– The purchase of mortgage-backed securities issued by government-sponsored enterprises such as Ginnie Mae, Fannie Mae or Freddie Mac. Basis Point (bps)– One hundredth of one percent, used chiefly in expressing differences of interest rates. BOJ (Bank of Japan)– Japanese central bank. Central Bank– A monopolized and often nationalized institution given privileged control over the production and distribution of money and credit. CLO (Collateralized Loan Obligations)– A special purpose vehicle (SPV) with securitization payments in the form of different tranches. Financial institutions back this security with receivables from loans. CMBS (Commercial Mortgage-Backed Securities)– A debt obligation that represents claims to the cash flows from pools of mortgage loans on commercial property. Collateral– Property or other assets that a borrower offers a lender to secure a loan. Corporate – Of or relating to a bond issued by a corporation as opposed to a bond issued by the U.S. Treasury, a non-U.S. government or a municipality. Corporate Credit– A term that is used in written investment materials and commentaries to refer to a corporation’s debt or to the corporate debt market as a whole. Developed Markets– Countries that have sound, well-established economies and are therefore thought to offer safer, more stable investment opportunities than developing markets. Duration– A measure of the sensitivity of the price (the value of principal) of a fixed-income investment to a change in interest rates. Duration is expressed as a number of years. Rising interest rates mean falling bond prices, while declining interest rates mean rising bond prices. ECB (European Central Bank)– The European Central Bank (ECB) is the central bank responsible for monetary policy of the European Union (EU) member countries that have adopted the euro currency. This currency union is known as the eurozone and currently includes 19 countries. The ECB’s primary objective is price stability in the euro area. Emerging Markets Credits– Emerging markets bonds (debt). Federal Reserve (the Fed)– The central bank of the United States which regulates the U.S. monetary and financial system. FFELP (Federal Family Education Loan Program)– Student loans made through this program are funded by private lenders and are insured by guaranty agencies and reinsured by the federal government. Fiscal Stimulus– An increase in public spending or a reduction in the level of taxation that might be performed by a government in order to encourage and support economic growth. GBP– British pound. Growth– A diversified portfolio of stocks that has capital appreciation as its primary goal, with little or no dividend payouts. Portfolio companies would mainly consist of companies with above-average growth in earnings that reinvest their earnings into expansion, acquisitions, and/or research and development. Hawkishness– Generally favoring relatively high interest rates in order to keep inflation in check. Inflation– A condition of a rise in the general level of prices of goods and services in an economy over a period of time. Inflationary– Of, associated with, or tending to cause inflation. Investment Grade– A bond that is rated Baa3/BBB- or higher by Moody’s, Standard & Poors and Fitch. Liquidity– The ability to convert an asset to cash quickly. Macroeconomic– Relating to the branch of economics concerned with large-scale or general economic factors, such as interest rates and national productivity. MBS (Mortgage-Backed Securities)– A type of asset-backed security that is secured by a mortgage or collection of mortgages. These securities must also be grouped in one of the top two ratings as determined by an accredited credit rating agency, and usually pay periodic payments that are similar to coupon payments. Furthermore, the mortgage must have originated from a regulated and authorized financial institution. Monetary Policy– The actions of a central bank, currency board or other regulatory committee that determine the size and rate of growth of the money supply, which in turn affects interest rates. Outperform– Outperform is when an investment is expected to perform better than the return generated by a particular index or the overall market. Since the performance of many investments is compared to a benchmark index, outperform refers to generating a higher return than a particular benchmark over time. Outperform also refers to an analyst’s rating on a security, and outperform is a better rating than neutral and worse than a strong buy recommendation. Overweight – A condition where the portfolio exposure to a given asset class (or risk measure) exceeds that of the benchmark index. PBOC (People’s Bank of China)– China central bank. Risk-Off– When stocks are selling off and investors run for shelter in bonds or gold, the environment is said to be risk-off. Securitized– When a bank pools together different kinds of loans to clear them off its balance sheet, to free up credit for new lenders, and to create new securities that can be marketed and sold to investors. Single-asset, single-borrower (SASB)– SASB conduit loans consist of one, large loan for a single property that is securitized and sold on the secondary market. Sovereign– A national government within a given country. Spread– The difference between the bid and the ask price of a security or asset. Stimulus– Something that causes a reaction; an incentive. Tariff– A tax imposed on imported goods and services. Tightening– Short for tight monetary policy. A situation in which a central bank enacts relatively high target interest rates to lower the available of credit. Effectively “tightening” the supply of credit. Underperformance– When an investment is underperforming, it is not keeping pace with other securities or indices. Underweight– A condition where a portfolio does not hold a sufficient amount of a particular security when compared to the security’s weight in the underlying benchmark portfolio. U.S. Treasury– The U.S. Treasury is the government department responsible for issuing all Treasury bonds, notes and bills. Valuations– The process of determining the current worth of an asset or company. There are many techniques that can be used to determine value, some are subjective and others are objective. Volatility– A measure of the risk of price moves for a security calculated from the standard deviation of day to day logarithmic historical price changes. Yield– The income return on an investment. This refers to the interest or dividends received from a security and is usually expressed annually as a percentage based on the investment’s cost, its current market value or its face value. Yield Curve– A curve on a graph in which the yield of fixed-interest securities is plotted against the length of time they have to run to maturity. Yield Premium– Additional return.
